title: Lemma A(iii): lower semicontinuity of Phi_n and limiting reduction to simple roots

comments.author: human
comments.body:
The reduction to the simple-root case uses the smoothing operator $T_\epsilon=(1-\epsilon\,d/dx)^n$ and requires a delicate limit $\epsilon\to0$.

**Key subtlety:** $\Phi_n$ is NOT continuous at multiple roots; it blows up to $+\infty$. The correct property is *lower semicontinuity* of $\Phi_n$ on $\mathbb R^n$ (extended-real valued), equivalently *upper semicontinuity* of $1/\Phi_n$.

**Required to prove:**
1. $\Phi_n$ is lower semicontinuous: on the open simple-root locus it is continuous; near any coalescence of roots at least one score $\to\pm\infty$, forcing $\Phi_n\to+\infty$.
2. $1/\Phi_n(T_\epsilon p)\to 1/\Phi_n(p)$ and $1/\Phi_n(T_\epsilon^2(p\boxplus_n q))\to 1/\Phi_n(p\boxplus_n q)$ as $\epsilon\to0$, **including the case where $p$ (or $q$) already has multiple roots** (then the corresponding RHS term is $0$ and only $\limsup$ control is needed).
3. Inequality direction: from $(\star)$ for $(T_\epsilon p,T_\epsilon q)$, pass to the limit taking $\limsup$ on the LHS (USC) and $\lim$ on the RHS.

**Verified numerically:** $T_\epsilon$ preserves real-rootedness and separates multiple roots into simple ones for $\epsilon>0$.
